text: In the 1970s, Ulric Neisser proposed the "perceptual cycle": a schema directs exploratory activity, which samples the environment, which updates the schema. What we attend to is never raw sensation — it is always mediated by the anticipatory schemas we carry. Crucially, schemas are themselves narrative structures: they encode not just objects but sequences, roles, and expected outcomes. A person operating under the schema of "busy professional running late" will literally not see the street musician who would otherwise captivate them. Attention is not a spotlight we aim; it is a story we are already inside.
question: According to Neisser's perceptual cycle, what role do schemas play in attention?

options.text: They suppress irrelevant sensory input at the retinal level before processing begins
They direct exploratory activity, shaping which aspects of the environment get sampled
They operate independently of narrative structures, filtering only spatial information
They slow attention to allow more thorough processing of each stimulus
